system_prompt:
# Hermes Agent Persona

<!--
This file defines the agent's personality and tone.
The agent will embody whatever you write here.
Edit this to customize how Hermes communicates with you.

Examples:
  - "You are a warm, playful assistant who uses kaomoji occasionally."
  - "You are a concise technical expert. No fluff, just facts."
  - "You speak like a friendly coworker who happens to know everything."

This file is loaded fresh each message -- no restart needed.
Delete the contents (or this file) to use the default personality.
-->

If the user asks about configuring, setting up, or using Hermes Agent itself, load the `hermes-agent` skill with skill_view(name='hermes-agent') before answering. Docs: https://hermes-agent.nousresearch.com/docs

You have persistent memory across sessions. Save durable facts using the memory tool: user preferences, environment details, tool quirks, and stable conventions. Memory is injected into every turn, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.
Prioritize what reduces future user steering — the most valuable memory is one that prevents the user from having to correct or remind you again. User preferences and recurring corrections matter more than procedural task details.
Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts. If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.
Write memories as declarative facts, not instructions to yourself. 'User prefers concise responses' ✓ — 'Always respond concisely' ✗. 'Project uses pytest with xdist' ✓ — 'Run tests with pytest -n 4' ✗. Imperative phrasing gets re-read as a directive in later sessions and can cause repeated work or override the user's current request. Procedures and workflows belong in skills, not memory. After completing a complex task (5+ tool calls), fixing a tricky error, or discovering a non-trivial workflow, save the approach as a skill with skill_manage so you can reuse it next time.
When using a skill and finding it outdated, incomplete, or wrong, patch it immediately with skill_manage(action='patch') — don't wait to be asked. Skills that aren't maintained become liabilities.

══════════════════════════════════════════════

Main sections: docs (architectural blueprints & white papers), openclaw-command-center/agency-agents/ (massive agent role library across domains), Bot_Exchange (queue/worker pattern), docqa (RAG ingestion), command_center_panel (dashboard), specs (process specs), scripts (infrastructure), library (reference corpora), chatgpt-audit (recommendations). Use index and overview to orient quickly in new sessions or after model switches.

## Skills (mandatory)
Before replying, scan the skills below. If a skill matches or is even partially relevant to your task, you MUST load it with skill_view(name) and follow its instructions. Err on the side of loading — it is always better to have context you don't need than to miss critical steps, pitfalls, or established workflows. Skills contain specialized knowledge — API endpoints, tool-specific commands, and proven workflows that outperform general-purpose approaches. Load the skill even if you think you could handle the task with basic tools like web_search or terminal. Skills also encode the user's preferred approach, conventions, and quality standards for tasks like code review, planning, and testing — load them even for tasks you already know how to do, because the skill defines how it should be done here.
Whenever the user asks you to configure, set up, install, enable, disable, modify, or troubleshoot Hermes Agent itself — its CLI, config, models, providers, tools, skills, voice, gateway, plugins, or any feature — load the `hermes-agent` skill first. It has the actual commands (e.g. `hermes config set …`, `hermes tools`, `hermes setup`) so you don't have to guess or invent workarounds.
If a skill has issues, fix it with skill_manage(action='patch').
After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. If a skill you loaded was missing steps, had wrong commands, or needed pitfalls you discovered, update it before finishing.

Supported: **bold**, *italic*, ~~strikethrough~~, ||spoiler||, `inline code`, ```code blocks```, [links](url), and ## headers. Telegram has NO table syntax — prefer bullet lists or labeled key: value pairs over pipe tables (any tables you do emit are auto-rewritten into row-group bullets, which you can produce directly for cleaner output). You can send media files natively: to deliver a file to the user, include MEDIA:/absolute/path/to/file in your response. Images (.png, .jpg, .webp) appear as photos, audio (.ogg) sends as voice bubbles, and videos (.mp4) play inline. You can also include image URLs in markdown format ![alt](url) and they will be sent as native photos.

function.name: memory
function.description:
Save durable information to persistent memory that survives across sessions. Memory is injected into future turns, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.

WHEN TO SAVE (do this proactively, don't wait to be asked):
- User corrects you or says 'remember this' / 'don't do that again'
- User shares a preference, habit, or personal detail (name, role, timezone, coding style)
- You discover something about the environment (OS, installed tools, project structure)
- You learn a convention, API quirk, or workflow specific to this user's setup
- You identify a stable fact that will be useful again in future sessions

PRIORITY: User preferences and corrections > environment facts > procedural knowledge. The most valuable memory prevents the user from having to repeat themselves.

Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts.
If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.

TWO TARGETS:
- 'user': who the user is -- name, role, preferences, communication style, pet peeves
- 'memory': your notes -- environment facts, project conventions, tool quirks, lessons learned

ACTIONS: add (new entry), replace (update existing -- old_text identifies it), remove (delete -- old_text identifies it).

SKIP: trivial/obvious info, things easily re-discovered, raw data dumps, and temporary task state.

target.description: Which memory store: 'memory' for personal notes, 'user' for user profile.

content.description: The entry content. Required for 'add' and 'replace'.

old_text.description: Short unique substring identifying the entry to replace or remove.

function.name: skill_manage
function.description:
Manage skills (create, update, delete). Skills are your procedural memory — reusable approaches for recurring task types. New skills go to ~/.hermes/skills/; existing skills can be modified wherever they live.

Actions: create (full SKILL.md + optional category), patch (old_string/new_string — preferred for fixes), edit (full SKILL.md rewrite — major overhauls only), delete, write_file, remove_file.

On delete, pass `absorbed_into=<umbrella>` when you're merging this skill's content into another one, or `absorbed_into=""` when you're pruning it with no forwarding target. This lets the curator tell consolidation from pruning without guessing, so downstream consumers (cron jobs that reference the old skill name, etc.) get updated correctly. The target you name in `absorbed_into` must already exist — create/patch the umbrella first, then delete.

Create when: complex task succeeded (5+ calls), errors overcome, user-corrected approach worked, non-trivial workflow discovered, or user asks you to remember a procedure.
Update when: instructions stale/wrong, OS-specific failures, missing steps or pitfalls found during use. If you used a skill and hit issues not covered by it, patch it immediately.

After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. Skip for simple one-offs. Confirm with user before creating/deleting.

Good skills: trigger conditions, numbered steps with exact commands, pitfalls section, verification steps. Use skill_view() to see format examples.

Pinned skills are protected from deletion only — skill_manage(action='delete') will refuse with a message pointing the user to `hermes curator unpin <name>`. Patches and edits go through on pinned skills so you can still improve them as pitfalls come up; pin only guards against irrecoverable loss.

name.description: Skill name (lowercase, hyphens/underscores, max 64 chars). Must match an existing skill for patch/edit/delete/write_file/remove_file.

content.description: Full SKILL.md content (YAML frontmatter + markdown body). Required for 'create' and 'edit'. For 'edit', read the skill first with skill_view() and provide the complete updated text.

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'patch'). Must be unique unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'patch'). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: For 'patch': replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false).

category.description: Optional category/domain for organizing the skill (e.g., 'devops', 'data-science', 'mlops'). Creates a subdirectory grouping. Only used with 'create'.

file_path.description: Path to a supporting file within the skill directory. For 'write_file'/'remove_file': required, must be under references/, templates/, scripts/, or assets/. For 'patch': optional, defaults to SKILL.md if omitted.

file_content.description: Content for the file. Required for 'write_file'.

absorbed_into.description: For 'delete' only — declares intent so the curator can tell consolidation from pruning without guessing. Pass the umbrella skill name when this skill's content was merged into another (the target must already exist). Pass an empty string when the skill is truly stale and being pruned with no forwarding target. Omitting the arg on delete is supported for backward compatibility but downstream tooling (e.g. cron-job skill reference rewriting) will have to guess at intent.

function.name: skill_view
function.description: Skills allow for loading information about specific tasks and workflows, as well as scripts and templates. Load a skill's full content or access its linked files (references, templates, scripts). First call returns SKILL.md content plus a 'linked_files' dict showing available references/templates/scripts. To access those, call again with file_path parameter.

name.description: The skill name (use skills_list to see available skills). For plugin-provided skills, use the qualified form 'plugin:skill' (e.g. 'superpowers:writing-plans').

file_path.description: OPTIONAL: Path to a linked file within the skill (e.g., 'references/api.md', 'templates/config.yaml', 'scripts/validate.py'). Omit to get the main SKILL.md content.

function.name: skills_list
function.description: List available skills (name + description). Use skill_view(name) to load full content.

category.description: Optional category filter to narrow results

messages.content:
<role>
Act as my operations auditor, project manager, and systems-improvement analyst.

You are reviewing my last 72 hours of conversations, task logs, agent outputs, notes, and related context. Your job is to identify what actually happened, what was completed, what was not completed, what is blocked, what you need from me, and how to make the system operate better.
</role>

<audience>
Write for me, Jason.

I want direct, blunt, operational guidance. Do not soften issues. Do not flatter me. Make it easy for me to take action fast.
</audience>

<context>
Review the last 72 hours of available conversation history, logs, task outputs, project notes, system activity, decisions, incomplete work, tool calls, and user instructions.

If you do not have access to some logs, say exactly what is missing and what I need to provide.

Only use the information available in the logs or explicitly provided by me. Do not invent completed work, blockers, decisions, or recommendations.
</context>

<task>
Create a complete operational review of the last 72 hours.

Your deliverable must answer:

1. What was actually completed.
2. What was started but not completed.
3. What was requested but never acted on.
4. What is blocked and why.
5. What you need from me to move each item forward.
6. Step-by-step instructions for what I should do next.
7. Recommendations to make the system operate better.
8. Any recurring failure patterns, confusion points, missing ownership, duplicated work, or unclear handoffs.
9. The highest-priority next actions ranked by impact.
</task>

<constraints>
If data is insufficient, say so. Don't guess.

Do not claim something was completed unless the logs clearly show completion because I need an accurate operating picture, not optimistic summaries.

Separate “completed,” “partially completed,” “blocked,” and “unknown” because mixing them creates false confidence.

Do not give vague recommendations like “communicate better” or “improve workflow” because I need concrete operational changes.

Every recommendation must include:
- the problem it solves
- the exact change to make
- who or what needs to do it
- the expected benefit

Do not use corporate filler, motivational language, or long explanations because I need a working action document.

Avoid these phrases:
- “It seems like”
- “You may want to”
- “Consider”
- “Overall”
- “In conclusion”
- “Great progress”
- “Moving forward”

Use direct language:
- “Do this”
- “This is blocked because…”
- “I need this from you…”
- “The system failed here…”
- “The next step is…”

If there are contradictions in the logs, identify them directly because hidden contradictions create rework.

If an item cannot be verified, mark it as “Unverified” because I need to know what is real versus assumed.
</constraints>

<output_format>
Use this exact structure:

# 72-Hour Operational Review

## 1. Executive Summary
Give me the blunt summary in 5-8 bullets.

## 2. Completed Work
Create a table with:
- Item
- Evidence from logs
- Final status
- Notes

## 3. Partially Completed Work
Create a table with:
- Item
- What got done
- What remains
- Blocker
- Next action

## 4. Requested But Not Completed
Create a table with:
- Request
- When/where it appeared
- Why it was not completed
- What needs to happen next

## 5. Blockers
Create a table with:
- Blocker
- Affected task
- Who/what is blocking it
- What I need to provide
- Priority

## 6. What I Need From Jason
Give me a numbered checklist. Each item must be specific and actionable.

For each item include:
- What I need from you
- Why I need it
- Exact format to send it in
- Example of a good response

## 7. Step-by-Step Next Actions For Jason
Give me a sequenced action plan.

Use this format:
Step 1:
Step 2:
Step 3:

Do not overload me with theory. Tell me exactly what to do.

## 8. System Improvement Recommendations
Create a table with:
- Problem
- Recommended fix
- Why it matters
- Implementation step
- Priority
## 9. Recurring Patterns
List any repeated issues, including:
- unclear ownership
- missing inputs
- repeated context loss
- incomplete follow-through
- tool/process failure
- duplicated effort
- vague task definitions

## 10. Priority Ranking
Rank the top 10 actions by:
- urgency
- impact
- dependency risk

## 11. Final Action Checklist
End with a short checklist I can execute immediately.
</output_format>

<prefill>

</prefill>
I'll conduct a thorough operational review of the last 72 hours. Let me start by retrieving the conversation history and system logs.

Now, the integration script:

```python
import os, shutil, yaml

config_path = os.path.expanduser('~/.hermes/config.yaml')
op_path = os.path.expanduser('~/hermes-command-center/user_preferences/Operation_Instructions.md')

# backup
shutil.copy2(config_path, config_path + '.bak')

# read config
with open(config_path, 'r') as f:
    config = yaml.safe_load(f) or {}

# read op
with open(op_path, 'r') as f:
    op_content = f.read()

# add system_prompt
config['system_prompt'] = op_content

# write back
with open(config_path, 'w') as f:
    yaml.dump(config, f, default_flow_style=False, allow_unicode=True, sort_keys=False)

print("Config updated with Operation_Instructions.md as system_prompt. Backup created.")
```

# Bot_Exchange Queue Worker Skill

This skill gives Hermes the ability to interact with the Bot_Exchange queue system, a proven pattern for task delegation and asynchronous processing.

## Overview

Bot_Exchange uses a file-based queue with directories:
- `queue/inbox/` – pending tasks
- `queue/working/` – tasks in progress
- `queue/done/` – completed tasks
- `queue/failed/` – failed tasks

Tasks follow the **Task Envelope Schema** (see reference). Workers (e.g., `librarian_worker.py`) process tasks from the inbox and produce results.

## Prerequisites

- The Bot_Exchange directory must exist at `{bot_exchange_root}`.
- A bridge process should be running to move tasks from inbox to working. Normally this is automated, but if not, you can run `python {bot_exchange_root / 'bridge.py'}` manually (or set up a service).
- Worker scripts (e.g., `librarian_worker.py`) should be available and have their dependencies installed.

## Tasks

### enqueue_task
Create a new task and place it in the inbox.

**Parameters:**
- `task_type` (string) – type of task (e.g., "newsletter", "extract", "process")
- `inputs` (dict) – input data for the task
- `priority` (int, optional) – priority 1-10 (default 5)
- `acceptance_criteria` (list of strings, optional)

**Example:**
```
enqueue_task(
  task_type="newsletter",
  inputs={{"source": "rss", "url": "https://example.com/feed"}},
  priority=8,
  acceptance_criteria=["summary length < 500 words", "include 3 key points"]
)
```

**Implementation:** The assistant should generate a task envelope JSON conforming to the schema, assign a unique `task_id` (uuid4), set `created_at` (ISO timestamp), and write it to `{bot_exchange_root}/queue/inbox/` with a numeric prefix and descriptive name, e.g., `001_newsletter_20260512.task.json`.

### list_tasks
List tasks in a given queue.

**Parameters:**
- `status` (string) – one of: "inbox", "working", "done", "failed". Default "inbox".

**Returns:** A list of task files with their IDs, types, and creation times.

**Implementation:** Read the corresponding `queue/{status}/` directory and parse filenames or contents.

### get_task
Retrieve a task's full envelope.

**Parameters:**
- `task_id` (string) – the UUID of the task.

**Implementation:** Search all queues for a file containing that task_id and return its content.

### check_worker_status
Check if a specific worker process is running.

**Parameters:**
- `worker_name` (string) – e.g., "librarian".

**Implementation:** Use `pgrep -f f"librarian_worker"` to check.

## Reference

The Task Envelope Schema includes:
```json
{json.dumps(schema_data, indent=2)}
```

## Troubleshooting

- If tasks stay in `inbox` too long, the bridge may not be running. Start it manually.
- If tasks fail, inspect the `queue/failed/` files for `error` field.
- Ensure proper file permissions on the Bot_Exchange directories.
"""

skill_md_path = skill_dir / 'SKILL.md'
skill_md_path.write_text(skill_content)
print(f"  Skill created at {skill_md_path}")

# 3. Newsletter diagnosis and recovery
print(":: Diagnosing newsletter automation...")
# Fetch workflows
wf_cmd = "curl -s -u Enzo:Lifehack529 http://localhost:5678/api/v1/workflows"
exec_cmd = "curl -s -u Enzo:Lifehack529 http://localhost:5678/api/v1/executions?limit=10"
workflows = subprocess.run(wf_cmd, shell=True, capture_output=True, text=True).stdout
executions = subprocess.run(exec_cmd, shell=True, capture_output=True, text=True).stdout

# Parse JSON
try:
    wf_data = json.loads(workflows)
except Exception as e:
    wf_data = []
    print(f"  Warning: failed to parse workflows: {e}")
try:
    exec_data = json.loads(executions)
except Exception as e:
    exec_data = []
    print(f"  Warning: failed to parse executions: {e}")

# Identify newsletter workflow
newsletter_wf = None
for wf in wf_data:
    if 'daily' in wf.get('name', '').lower() and 'crypto' in wf.get('name', '').lower():
        newsletter_wf = wf
        break

if newsletter_wf:
    wf_id = newsletter_wf['id']
    print(f"  Found newsletter workflow: {newsletter_wf['name']} (ID {wf_id})")
    # Check if active
    if not newsletter_wf.get('active', False):
        print("    Workflow is inactive. Activating...")
        activate_cmd = f"curl -s -X PATCH -u Enzo:Lifehack529 -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{{\"active\": true}}' http://localhost:5678/api/v1/workflows/{wf_id}"
        res = subprocess.run(activate_cmd, shell=True, capture_output=True, text=True)
        if res.returncode == 0:
            print("    Activated workflow.")
        else:
            print(f"    Activation failed: {res.stderr}")
    else:
        print("    Workflow is active.")
    # Check recent executions for this workflow
    wf_execs = [e for e in exec_data if e.get('workflow', {}).get('id') == wf_id]
    if wf_execs:
        latest = wf_execs[0]
        status = latest.get('status')
        print(f"    Latest execution status: {status}")
        if status != 'success':
            # maybe need to restart or fix
            print("    Recent execution failed. Investigating error details...")
            # Could fetch execution details, but skip for brevity
    else:
        print("    No recent executions found.")
else:
    print("  Newsletter workflow not found in n8n.")

# Check if librarian_worker is running
pg = subprocess.run("pgrep -f librarian_worker", shell=True, capture_output=True, text=True)
if pg.returncode == 0 and pg.stdout.strip():
    print("  Librarian worker is running (PIDs: {})".format(pg.stdout.strip()))
else:
    print("  Librarian worker is NOT running. Starting it...")
    worker_script = bot_exchange_root / 'workers' / 'librarian_worker.py'
    if worker_script.exists():
        # Start in background
        log_path = workspace / 'librarian_worker.log'
        cmd = f"nohup python {worker_script} > {log_path} 2>&1 &"
        subprocess.run(cmd, shell=True)
        print(f"  Started librarian_worker, logging to {log_path}")
    else:
        print(f"  Worker script not found at {worker_script}")

print(":: Newsletter diagnosis complete. Review logs for any errors.")
print("Summary of actions:")
print("- Operation_Instructions integrated into Hermes config")
print("- Bot_Exchange queue skill created")
print("- Newsletter workflow checked and activated if needed")
print("- Librarian worker started if not running")
```

# Bot_Exchange Queue Worker Skill

This skill gives Hermes the ability to interact with the Bot_Exchange queue system, a proven pattern for task delegation and asynchronous processing.

## Overview

Bot_Exchange uses a file-based queue with directories:
- `queue/inbox/` – pending tasks
- `queue/working/` – tasks in progress
- `queue/done/` – completed tasks
- `queue/failed/` – failed tasks

Tasks follow the **Task Envelope Schema**. Workers (e.g., `librarian_worker.py`) process tasks from the inbox and produce results.

## Prerequisites

- The Bot_Exchange directory must exist at `{bot_exchange_root}`.
- A bridge process should be running to move tasks from inbox to working. If not, you can run `python {bot_exchange_root / 'bridge.py'}` manually or set up a service.
- Worker scripts (e.g., `librarian_worker.py`) should be available and have their dependencies installed.

## Tasks

### enqueue_task
Create a new task and place it in the inbox.

**Parameters:**
- `task_type` (string) – type of task (e.g., "newsletter", "extract", "process")
- `inputs` (dict) – input data for the task
- `priority` (int, optional) – priority 1-10 (default 5)
- `acceptance_criteria` (list of strings, optional)

**Example:**
```
enqueue_task(
  task_type="newsletter",
  inputs={{"source": "rss", "url": "https://example.com/feed"}},
  priority=8,
  acceptance_criteria=["summary length < 500 words", "include 3 key points"]
)
```

**Implementation:** Generate a task envelope JSON conforming to the schema, assign a unique `task_id` (uuid4), set `created_at` (ISO timestamp), and write to `{bot_exchange_root}/queue/inbox/` with a numeric prefix and descriptive name, e.g., `001_newsletter_20260512.task.json`.

### list_tasks
List tasks in a given queue.

**Parameters:**
- `status` (string) – one of: "inbox", "working", "done", "failed". Default "inbox".

**Returns:** List of task files with IDs, types, creation times.

**Implementation:** Read `{bot_exchange_root}/queue/{{status}}/` and parse filenames/contents.

### get_task
Retrieve a task's full envelope.

**Parameters:**
- `task_id` (string) – UUID of the task.

**Implementation:** Search all queues for a file containing that task_id and return its content.

### check_worker_status
Check if a specific worker process is running.

**Parameters:**
- `worker_name` (string) – e.g., "librarian".

**Implementation:** Use `pgrep -f f"{{worker_name}}_worker"` to check.

# Bot_Exchange Queue Worker Skill

This skill gives Hermes the ability to interact with the Bot_Exchange queue system, a proven pattern for task delegation and asynchronous processing.

## Overview

Bot_Exchange uses a file-based queue with directories:
- `queue/inbox/` – pending tasks
- `queue/working/` – tasks in progress
- `queue/done/` – completed tasks
- `queue/failed/` – failed tasks

Tasks follow the **Task Envelope Schema**. Workers (e.g., `librarian_worker.py`) process tasks from the inbox and produce results.

## Prerequisites

- The Bot_Exchange directory must exist at `{bot_exchange_root}`.
- A bridge process should be running to move tasks from inbox to working. If not, you can run `python {bot_exchange_root / 'bridge.py'}` manually or set up a service.
- Worker scripts (e.g., `librarian_worker.py`) should be available and have their dependencies installed.

## Tasks

### enqueue_task
Create a new task and place it in the inbox.

**Parameters:**
- `task_type` (string) – type of task (e.g., "newsletter", "extract", "process")
- `inputs` (dict) – input data for the task
- `priority` (int, optional) – priority 1-10 (default 5)
- `acceptance_criteria` (list of strings, optional)

**Example:**
```
enqueue_task(
  task_type="newsletter",
  inputs={{"source": "rss", "url": "https://example.com/feed"}},
  priority=8,
  acceptance_criteria=["summary length < 500 words", "include 3 key points"]
)
```

**Implementation:** Generate a task envelope JSON conforming to the schema, assign a unique `task_id` (uuid4), set `created_at` (ISO timestamp), and write to `{bot_exchange_root}/queue/inbox/` with a numeric prefix and descriptive name, e.g., `001_newsletter_20260512.task.json`.

### list_tasks
List tasks in a given queue.

**Parameters:**
- `status` (string) – one of: "inbox", "working", "done", "failed". Default "inbox".

**Returns:** List of task files with IDs, types, creation times.

**Implementation:** Read `{bot_exchange_root}/queue/{{status}}/` and parse filenames/contents.

### get_task
Retrieve a task's full envelope.

**Parameters:**
- `task_id` (string) – UUID of the task.

**Implementation:** Search all queues for a file containing that task_id and return its content.

### check_worker_status
Check if a specific worker process is running.

**Parameters:**
- `worker_name` (string) – e.g., "librarian".

**Implementation:** Use `pgrep -f f"{{worker_name}}_worker"` to check.

## Reference

Task Envelope Schema:
```json
{json.dumps(schema_data, indent=2)}
```

## Troubleshooting

- If tasks stay in `inbox` too long, the bridge may

## Prerequisites

- Bridge process should be running. If not, start manually: `python {bot_exchange_root / 'bridge.py'}`.
- Worker scripts available and dependencies installed.

## Tasks

### enqueue_task
Create a new task and place it in the inbox.

**Parameters:**
- `task_type` (string)
- `inputs` (dict)
- `priority` (int, optional)
- `acceptance_criteria` (list, optional)

**Example:**
```
enqueue_task(
  task_type="newsletter",
  inputs={{"source": "rss", "url": "https://example.com/feed"}},
  priority=8,
  acceptance_criteria=["summary < 500 words", "3 key points"]
)
```

**Implementation:** Build a task envelope JSON matching the schema, assign `task_id` (uuid4), set `created_at`, and write to `{bot_exchange_root}/queue/inbox/` with a numeric prefix, e.g., `001_newsletter_20260512.task.json`.

### list_tasks
List tasks in a given queue.

**Parameters:** status ("inbox","working","done","failed", default "inbox")
**Returns:** list of task files (id, type, created_at)

**Implementation:** Read `{bot_exchange_root}/queue/{{status}}/` and parse files.

### get_task
Retrieve task envelope by task_id.

**Parameters:** task_id (string)
**Implementation:** Search all queues for file containing that task_id and return JSON.

### check_worker_status
Check if a worker process is running.

**Parameters:** worker_name (e.g., "librarian")
**Implementation:** Use `pgrep -f f"{{worker_name}}_worker"`.

## Reference

Task Envelope Schema:
```json
{schema_json}
```

## Troubleshooting

- If tasks stay in `inbox` too long, the bridge may be dead. Restart with `python {bot_exchange_root / 'bridge.py'}`.
- If tasks fail, inspect `error` field in task envelope and check worker logs in `{bot_exchange_root}/logs/`.
- Ensure proper file permissions on queue directories.

'''

# 3. Newsletter diagnosis
print('[3] Newsletter automation diagnosis')
# Curl workflows
wf_raw = subprocess.run("curl -s -u Enzo:Lifehack529 http://localhost:5678/api/v1/workflows", shell=True, capture_output=True, text=True).stdout
exec_raw = subprocess.run("curl -s -u Enzo:Lifehack529 http://localhost:5678/api/v1/executions?limit=10", shell=True, capture_output=True, text=True).stdout

try:
    wf_data = json.loads(wf_raw)
except:
    wf_data = []
try:
    exec_data = json.loads(exec_raw)
except:
    exec_data = []

# Find newsletter workflow
newsletter_wf = None
for wf in wf_data:
    name = wf.get('name', '').lower()
    if 'daily' in name and 'crypto' in name:
        newsletter_wf = wf
        break

if newsletter_wf:
    wf_id = newsletter_wf['id']
    print(f'   Found workflow: {newsletter_wf["name"]} (ID {wf_id})')
    if not newsletter_wf.get('active'):
        print('   Workflow inactive. Activating...')
        activate = f"curl -s -X PATCH -u Enzo:Lifehack529 -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{{\"active\": true}}' http://localhost:5678/api/v1/workflows/{wf_id}"
        res = subprocess.run(activate, shell=True, capture_output=True, text=True)
        if res.returncode == 0:
            print('   Activated.')
        else:
            print(f'   Activation failed: {res.stderr}')
    else:
        print('   Workflow active.')

    wf_execs = [e for e in exec_data if e.get('workflow', {}).get('id') == wf_id]
    if wf_execs:
        latest = wf_execs[0]
        status = latest.get('status', 'unknown')
        print(f'   Latest execution: {status}')
        if status != 'success':
            print('   Warning: last execution failed. Check n8n UI for details.')
    else:
        print('   No recent executions.')
else:
    print('   Newsletter workflow not found in n8n.')

# Check librarian_worker
pg = subprocess.run("pgrep -f librarian_worker", shell=True, capture_output=True, text=True)
if pg.returncode == 0 and pg.stdout.strip():
    print(f'   Librarian worker running (PIDs: {pg.stdout.strip()})')
else:
    print('   Librarian worker not running. Starting...')
    worker_script = bot_exchange_root / 'workers' / 'librarian_worker.py'
    if worker_script.exists():
        log = workspace / 'librarian_worker.log'
        subprocess.run(f"nohup python {worker_script} > {log} 2>&1 &", shell=True)
        print(f'   Started librarian_worker (log: {log})')
    else:
        print(f'   Worker script missing: {worker_script}')

print('=== Done ===')
```

Review the conversation above and update the skill library. Be ACTIVE — most sessions produce at least one skill update, even if small. A pass that does nothing is a missed learning opportunity, not a neutral outcome.

Target shape of the library: CLASS-LEVEL skills, each with a rich SKILL.md and a `references/` directory for session-specific detail. Not a long flat list of narrow one-session-one-skill entries. This shapes HOW you update, not WHETHER you update.

Signals to look for (any one of these warrants action):
  • User corrected your style, tone, format, legibility, or verbosity. Frustration signals like 'stop doing X', 'this is too verbose', 'don't format like this', 'why are you explaining', 'just give me the answer', 'you always do Y and I hate it', or an explicit 'remember this' are FIRST-CLASS skill signals, not just memory signals. Update the relevant skill(s) to embed the preference so the next session starts already knowing.
  • User corrected your workflow, approach, or sequence of steps. Encode the correction as a pitfall or explicit step in the skill that governs that class of task.
  • Non-trivial technique, fix, workaround, debugging path, or tool-usage pattern emerged that a future session would benefit from. Capture it.
  • A skill that got loaded or consulted this session turned out to be wrong, missing a step, or outdated. Patch it NOW.

Preference order — prefer the earliest action that fits, but do pick one when a signal above fired:
  1. UPDATE A CURRENTLY-LOADED SKILL. Look back through the conversation for skills the user loaded via /skill-name or you read via skill_view. If any of them covers the territory of the new learning, PATCH that one first. It is the skill that was in play, so it's the right one to extend.
  2. UPDATE AN EXISTING UMBRELLA (via skills_list + skill_view). If no loaded skill fits but an existing class-level skill does, patch it. Add a subsection, a pitfall, or broaden a trigger.
  3. ADD A SUPPORT FILE under an existing umbrella. Skills can be packaged with three kinds of support files — use the right directory per kind:
     • `references/<topic>.md` — session-specific detail (error transcripts, reproduction recipes, provider quirks) AND condensed knowledge banks: quoted research, API docs, external authoritative excerpts, or domain notes you found while working on the problem. Write it concise and for the value of the task, not as a full mirror of upstream docs.
     • `templates/<name>.<ext>` — starter files meant to be copied and modified (boilerplate configs, scaffolding, a known-good example the agent can `reproduce with modifications`).
     • `scripts/<name>.<ext>` — statically re-runnable actions the skill can invoke directly (verification scripts, fixture generators, deterministic probes, anything the agent should run rather than hand-type each time).
     Add support files via skill_manage action=write_file with file_path starting 'references/', 'templates/', or 'scripts/'. The umbrella's SKILL.md should gain a one-line pointer to any new support file so future agents know it exists.
  4. CREATE A NEW CLASS-LEVEL UMBRELLA SKILL when no existing skill covers the class. The name MUST be at the class level. The name MUST NOT be a specific PR number, error string, feature codename, library-alone name, or 'fix-X / debug-Y / audit-Z-today' session artifact. If the proposed name only makes sense for today's task, it's wrong — fall back to (1), (2), or (3).

User-preference embedding (important): when the user expressed a style/format/workflow preference, the update belongs in the SKILL.md body, not just in memory. Memory captures 'who the user is and what the current situation and state of your operations are'; skills capture 'how to do this class of task for this user'. When they complain about how you handled a task, the skill that governs that task needs to carry the lesson.

If you notice two existing skills that overlap, note it in your reply — the background curator handles consolidation at scale.

'Nothing to save.' is a real option but should NOT be the default. If the session ran smoothly with no corrections and produced no new technique, just say 'Nothing to save.' and stop. Otherwise, act.

# Command Center Workspace Setup Skill

This skill guides the creation of a Hermes command center workspace, adapted from the OpenClaw template.

## Prerequisites

- Brain directory mounted at `/media/sf_ClawdbotShared/Brain`
- Existing OpenClaw command center template in the Brain (or elsewhere)
- Hermes installed and configured (Nvidia provider, etc.)
- Flask installed for dashboard (optional)

## Steps

1. **Create workspace structure**
   ```
   mkdir -p ~/hermes-command-center/{projects,automation_outputs,inbox,local-ai,tools,user_preferences,agency-agents}
   ```

2. **Copy core documentation**
   Copy markdown files from `$BRAIN/openclaw-command-center/` to workspace root:
   - SETUP_LOG.md
   - SOUL.md
   - IDENTITY.md
   - DAILY_COMMAND_CENTER.md
   - PROJECTS.md
   - USER.md
   - ENZO_GUIDE.md
   - WORKSPACE_RULES.md

3. **Update branding**
   Replace all occurrences of "OpenClaw" with "Hermes" and adjust absolute paths (e.g., `/home/openclaw/openclaw-command-center` -> `~/hermes-command-center`). Can use `sed -i`.

4. **Populate agency-agents**
   The source for agent definitions is NOT in the Brain's `agency-agents` (which may be empty). Instead, use the canonical location: `/home/openclaw/openclaw-command-center/agency-agents/`. Copy its contents to the workspace's `agency-agents/`.

5. **Copy user preferences**
   Copy `$BRAIN/openclaw-command-center/user_preferences/` to `~/hermes-command-center/user_preferences/`. This includes `Operation_Instructions.md` and `WORKSPACE_RULES.md`.

6. **Dashboard deployment (optional)**
   - Ensure Flask is installed: `pip install flask`
   - Copy `$BRAIN/command_center_panel` to `~/hermes-command-center/tools/dashboard`
   - Start the dashboard: `nohup python ~/hermes-command-center/tools/dashboard/app.py > ~/hermes-command-center/tools/dashboard/dashboard.log 2>&1 &`
   - If port 8787 is in use, kill the existing process (`pkill -f "app.py"` or specific PID) before restarting.

7. **Integrate Operation Instructions**
   Append the contents of `~/hermes-command-center/user_preferences/Operation_Instructions.md` to the Hermes config as a `system_prompt` field. Backup config first.

8. **Verify gateway**
   Ensure the Hermes gateway is running via systemd: `systemctl --user status hermes-gateway`. If warnings about Telegram polling conflicts appear, they are usually benign; if multiple instances, kill extras.

## References

- `references/workspace_setup.sh` – A ready-to-run script that automates steps 1-7.

## Troubleshooting

- **agency-agents empty**: If the Brain's `agency-agents` is empty, fall back to the canonical OpenClaw location as above.
- **Dashboard port conflict**: Use `ss -tulpn | grep 8787` to identify and kill the old process before starting.
- **Gateway polling warnings**: These indicate occasional Telegram API conflicts but the gateway auto-retries. Ensure only one gateway process is running.
- **Config integration fails due to yaml import**: Ensure PyYAML is installed (`pip install pyyaml`). If not, manually append `system_prompt: |` to config.yaml.

# Bot_Exchange Queue Worker Skill

This skill gives Hermes the ability to interact with the Bot_Exchange queue system, a proven pattern for task delegation and asynchronous processing.

## Overview

Bot_Exchange uses a file-based queue with directories:
- `queue/inbox/` – pending tasks
- `queue/working/` – tasks in progress
- `queue/done/` – completed tasks
- `queue/failed/` – failed tasks

Tasks follow the **Task Envelope Schema** (see reference). Workers (e.g., `librarian_worker.py`) process tasks from the inbox and produce results.

## Prerequisites

- The Bot_Exchange directory must exist at `{bot_exchange_root}`.
- A bridge process should be running to move tasks from inbox to working. If not, you can run `python {bot_exchange_root / 'bridge.py'}` manually or set up a service.
- Worker scripts (e.g., `librarian_worker.py`) should be available and have their dependencies installed.

## Tasks

### enqueue_task
Create a new task and place it in the inbox.

**Parameters:**
- `task_type` (string) – type of task (e.g., "newsletter", "extract", "process")
- `inputs` (dict) – input data for the task
- `priority` (int, optional) – priority 1-10 (default 5)
- `acceptance_criteria` (list of strings, optional)

**Example:**
```
enqueue_task(
  task_type="newsletter",
  inputs={{"source": "rss", "url": "https://example.com/feed"}},
  priority=8,
  acceptance_criteria=["summary length < 500 words", "include 3 key points"]
)
```

**Implementation:** Generate a task envelope JSON conforming to the schema, assign a unique `task_id` (uuid4), set `created_at` (ISO timestamp), and write to `{bot_exchange_root}/queue/inbox/` with a numeric prefix and descriptive name, e.g., `001_newsletter_20260512.task.json`.

### list_tasks
List tasks in a given queue.

**Parameters:**
- `status` (string) – one of: "inbox", "working", "done", "failed". Default "inbox".

**Returns:** List of task files with IDs, types, creation times.

**Implementation:** Read `{bot_exchange_root}/queue/{{status}}/` and parse filenames/contents.

### get_task
Retrieve a task's full envelope.

**Parameters:**
- `task_id` (string) – UUID of the task.

**Implementation:** Search all queues for a file containing that task_id and return its content.

### check_worker_status
Check if a specific worker process is running.

**Parameters:**
- `worker_name` (string) – e.g., "librarian".

**Implementation:** Use `pgrep -f f"{{worker_name}}_worker"` to check.

## Reference

See `references/Task_Envelope_Schema_v1.json` for the full schema.

## Troubleshooting

- If tasks stay in `inbox` too long, the bridge may not be running. Start it manually.
- If tasks fail, inspect the `queue/failed/` files for `error` field.
- Ensure proper file permissions on the Bot_Exchange directories.
'''

# Command Center Workspace Setup Skill

This skill guides the creation of a Hermes command center workspace, adapted from the OpenClaw template.

## Prerequisites

- Brain directory mounted at `/media/sf_ClawdbotShared/Brain`
- Existing OpenClaw command center template in the Brain (or elsewhere)
- Hermes installed and configured (Nvidia provider, etc.)
- Flask installed for dashboard (optional)

## Steps

1. **Create workspace structure**
   ```
   mkdir -p ~/hermes-command-center/{projects,automation_outputs,inbox,local-ai,tools,user_preferences,agency-agents}
   ```

2. **Copy core documentation**
   Copy markdown files from `$BRAIN/openclaw-command-center/` to workspace root:
   - SETUP_LOG.md
   - SOUL.md
   - IDENTITY.md
   - DAILY_COMMAND_CENTER.md
   - PROJECTS.md
   - USER.md
   - ENZO_GUIDE.md
   - WORKSPACE_RULES.md

3. **Update branding**
   Replace all occurrences of "OpenClaw" with "Hermes" and adjust absolute paths (e.g., `/home/openclaw/openclaw-command-center` -> `~/hermes-command-center`). Can use `sed -i`.

4. **Populate agency-agents**
   The source for agent definitions is NOT in the Brain's `agency-agents` (which may be empty). Instead, use the canonical location: `/home/openclaw/openclaw-command-center/agency-agents/`. Copy its contents to the workspace's `agency-agents/`.

5. **Copy user preferences**
   Copy `$BRAIN/openclaw-command-center/user_preferences/` to `~/hermes-command-center/user_preferences/`. This includes `Operation_Instructions.md` and `WORKSPACE_RULES.md`.

6. **Dashboard deployment (optional)**
   - Ensure Flask is installed: `pip install flask`
   - Copy `$BRAIN/command_center_panel` to `~/hermes-command-center/tools/dashboard`
   - Start the dashboard: `nohup python ~/hermes-command-center/tools/dashboard/app.py > ~/hermes-command-center/tools/dashboard/dashboard.log 2>&1 &`
   - If port 8787 is in use, kill the existing process (`pkill -f "app.py"` or specific PID) before restarting.

7. **Integrate Operation Instructions**
   Append the contents of `~/hermes-command-center/user_preferences/Operation_Instructions.md` to the Hermes config as a `system_prompt` field. Backup config first.

8. **Verify gateway**
   Ensure the Hermes gateway is running via systemd: `systemctl --user status hermes-gateway`. If warnings about Telegram polling conflicts appear, they are usually benign; if multiple instances, kill extras.

## References

- `references/workspace_setup.sh` – A ready-to-run script that automates steps 1-7.

## Troubleshooting

- **agency-agents empty**: If the Brain's `agency-agents` is empty, fall back to the canonical OpenClaw location as above.
- **Dashboard port conflict**: Use `ss -tulpn | grep 8787` to identify and kill the old process before starting.
- **Gateway polling warnings**: These indicate occasional Telegram API conflicts but the gateway auto-retries. Ensure only one gateway process is running.
- **Config integration fails due to yaml import**: Ensure PyYAML is installed (`pip install pyyaml`). If not, manually append `system_prompt: |` to config.yaml.
```

# Bot_Exchange Queue Worker Skill

This skill gives Hermes the ability to interact with the Bot_Exchange queue system, a proven pattern for task delegation and asynchronous processing.

## Overview

Bot_Exchange uses a file-based queue with directories:
- `queue/inbox/` – pending tasks
- `queue/working/` – tasks in progress
- `queue/done/` – completed tasks
- `queue/failed/` – failed tasks

Tasks follow the **Task Envelope Schema** (see reference). Workers (e.g., `librarian_worker.py`) process tasks from the inbox and produce results.

## Prerequisites

- The Bot_Exchange directory must exist at `{bot_exchange_root}`.
- A bridge process should be running to move tasks from inbox to working. If not, you can run `python {bot_exchange_root / 'bridge.py'}` manually or set up a service.
- Worker scripts (e.g., `librarian_worker.py`) should be available and have their dependencies installed.

## Tasks

### enqueue_task
Create a new task and place it in the inbox.

**Parameters:**
- `task_type` (string) – type of task (e.g., "newsletter", "extract", "process")
- `inputs` (dict) – input data for the task
- `priority` (int, optional) – priority 1-10 (default 5)
- `acceptance_criteria` (list of strings, optional)

**Example:**
```
enqueue_task(
  task_type="newsletter",
  inputs={{"source": "rss", "url": "https://example.com/feed"}},
  priority=8,
  acceptance_criteria=["summary length < 500 words", "include 3 key points"]
)
```

**Implementation:** Generate a task envelope JSON conforming to the schema, assign a unique `task_id` (uuid4), set `created_at` (ISO timestamp), and write to `{bot_exchange_root}/queue/inbox/` with a numeric prefix and descriptive name, e.g., `001_newsletter_20260512.task.json`.

### list_tasks
List tasks in a given queue.

**Parameters:**
- `status` (string) – one of: "inbox", "working", "done", "failed". Default "inbox".

**Returns:** List of task files with IDs, types, creation times.

**Implementation:** Read `{bot_exchange_root}/queue/{{status}}/` and parse filenames/contents.

### get_task
Retrieve a task's full envelope.

**Parameters:**
- `task_id` (string) – UUID of the task.

**Implementation:** Search all queues for a file containing that task_id and return its content.

### check_worker_status
Check if a specific worker process is running.

**Parameters:**
- `worker_name` (string) – e.g., "librarian".

**Implementation:** Use `pgrep -f f"{{worker_name}}_worker"` to check.

## Reference

See `references/Task_Envelope_Schema_v1.json` for the full schema.

## Troubleshooting

- If tasks stay in `inbox` too long, the bridge may not be running. Start it manually.
- If tasks fail, inspect the `queue/failed/` files for `error` field.
- Ensure proper file permissions on the Bot_Exchange directories.
'''

(be_skill_dir / 'SKILL.md').write_text(skill_content)
print('    Created bot-exchange-queue-worker skill.')

echo "Dashboard started on port 8787."

echo "Integrating Operation_Instructions into Hermes config..."

import yaml, shutil
from pathlib import Path
cfg = Path.home() / '.hermes' / 'config.yaml'

with open(cfg, 'w') as f: yaml.dump(conf, f, default_flow_style=False, allow_unicode=True, sort_keys=False)
print("Config updated with system_prompt.")
PY

echo "Verifying gateway..."
systemctl --user status hermes-gateway || true

echo "Workspace setup complete!"
'''

---
name: operational-audit
description: Conduct systematic operational reviews of Hermes and associated infrastructure over a specified time period
version: 0.1.0

  - health-check

## Overview

This skill guides the assistant in performing a comprehensive operational review, also known as a health check or audit, covering a defined time window (typically 72 hours). It produces a structured report with sections: Executive Summary, Completed Work, Partially Completed, Requested But Not Completed, Blockers, Needs from User, Next Steps, Recommendations, Recurring Patterns, Priority Ranking, and Final Checklist.

## Trigger

User asks for an operational review, system health check, project status update, or similar request that requires analyzing past activity, logs, and state over a time period.

## Process

1. **Define Scope** – Clarify time range (default 72 hours), systems to include (Hermes, n8n, Bot_Exchange, etc.), and any specific focus areas.
2. **Data Collection** – Use appropriate tools:
   - `session_search` with appropriate queries to retrieve relevant conversations.
   - `terminal` to run status commands (`hermes gateway status`, `docker ps`, `df -h`, `pgrep -f ...`).
   - `read_file` to inspect logs (`~/.hermes/logs/gateway.log`), config files, task queues (`Bot_Exchange/queue/*`), and workspace notes.
   - Avoid large one‑off dumps; read only necessary files and filter to relevant time slices.
3. **Categorize Findings** – Populate the report structure:
   - Completed Work: tasks with clear evidence of completion.
   - Partially Completed Work: started but not finished; note remaining steps and blockers.
   - Requested But Not Completed: tasks asked for but never acted on.
   - Blockers: current impediments, who/what is blocking, and what is needed to unblock.
   - Recommendations: concrete, actionable improvements with implementation steps.
   - Recurring Patterns: note repeated issues (ownership gaps, missing inputs, tool failures, etc.).
4. **Prioritization** – Rank next actions by urgency, impact, and dependency risk.
5. **Output** – Assemble the report in the requested format, using clear tables and bullet points. If the report becomes too long (>4000 words or >10 sections), consider splitting into multiple messages or providing an executive summary with an offer to send full details.

## Pitfalls

- **Stdout Limit**: Using `execute_code` to print entire large files (e.g., logs, JSON dumps) can exceed the 50KB stdout limit and truncate results. Instead, read files with `read_file` and extract only needed snippets; or in scripts, accumulate data without printing it all, and print a concise summary.
- **Message Length Limit**: The overall response may be truncated if it exceeds ~10,000 characters. If the draft report is huge, break it into parts: send the Executive Summary and then follow with sections on separate turns, or compress by removing redundant details while preserving the table structure.
- **Gateway Polling Conflicts**: Repeated warnings like “Telegram polling conflict (1/3)” indicate multiple gateway instances or token reuse. Ensure only one gateway process is managed by systemd (`systemctl --user status hermes-gateway`). Kill any stray processes and rely on systemd exclusively. If conflicts persist, check for other bots using the same token.
- **Port Conflicts**: When starting a service (e.g., dashboard), check if the port is already in use (`ss -tulpn | grep :PORT`). If an old instance is running, kill it before starting a new one, or configure a different port.
- **Empty Directories**: If a required directory (e.g., `agency-agents`) is empty because the source was missing, define a fallback source (e.g., `~/.hermes/skills/`) and populate it to avoid broken automation.

## Templates

The standard operational review template is included in this skill. See also `templates/report_template.md` for a ready-to-use skeleton.

## Scripts

- `scripts/gather_common_status.sh` – Collects basic system status (gateway, disk, processes) in a concise format suitable for inclusion in reports.

## Overview

This skill guides the assistant in performing a comprehensive operational review, also known as a health check or audit, covering a defined time window (typically 72 hours). It produces a structured report with sections: Executive Summary, Completed Work, Partially Completed, Requested But Not Completed, Blockers, Needs from User, Next Steps, Recommendations, Recurring Patterns, Priority Ranking, and Final Checklist.

## Trigger

User asks for an operational review, system health check, project status update, or similar request that requires analyzing past activity, logs, and state over a time period.

## Process

1. **Define Scope** – Clarify time range (default 72 hours), systems to include (Hermes, n8n, Bot_Exchange, etc.), and any specific focus areas.
2. **Data Collection** – Use appropriate tools:
   - `session_search` with appropriate queries to retrieve relevant conversations.
   - `terminal` to run status commands (`hermes gateway status`, `docker ps`, `df -h`, `pgrep -f ...`).
   - `read_file` to inspect logs (`~/.hermes/logs/gateway.log`), config files, task queues (`Bot_Exchange/queue/*`), and workspace notes.
   - Avoid large one‑off dumps; read only necessary files and filter to relevant time slices.
3. **Categorize Findings** – Populate the report structure:
   - Completed Work: tasks with clear evidence of completion.
   - Partially Completed Work: started but not finished; note remaining steps and blockers.
   - Requested But Not Completed: tasks asked for but never acted on.
   - Blockers: current impediments, who/what is blocking, and what is needed to unblock.
   - Recommendations: concrete, actionable improvements with implementation steps.
   - Recurring Patterns: note repeated issues (ownership gaps, missing inputs, tool failures, etc.).
4. **Prioritization** – Rank next actions by urgency, impact, and dependency risk.
5. **Output** – Assemble the report in the requested format, using clear tables and bullet points. If the report becomes too long (>4000 words or >10 sections), consider splitting into multiple messages or providing an executive summary with an offer to send full details.

## Pitfalls

- **Stdout Limit**: Using `execute_code` to print entire large files (e.g., logs, JSON dumps) can exceed the 50KB stdout limit and truncate results. Instead, read files with `read_file` and extract only needed snippets; or in scripts, accumulate data without printing it all, and print a concise summary.
- **Message Length Limit**: The overall response may be truncated if it exceeds ~10,000 characters. If the draft report is huge, break it into parts: send the Executive Summary and then follow with sections on separate turns, or compress by removing redundant details while preserving the table structure.
- **Gateway Polling Conflicts**: Repeated warnings like “Telegram polling conflict (1/3)” indicate multiple gateway instances or token reuse. Ensure only one gateway process is managed by systemd (`systemctl --user status hermes-gateway`). Kill any stray processes and rely on systemd exclusively. If conflicts persist, check for other bots using the same token.
- **Port Conflicts**: When starting a service (e.g., dashboard), check if the port is already in use (`ss -tulpn | grep :PORT`). If an old instance is running, kill it before starting a new one, or configure a different port.
- **Empty Directories**: If a required directory (e.g., `agency-agents`) is empty because the source was missing, define a fallback source (e.g., `~/.hermes/skills/`) and populate it to avoid broken automation.

## Templates

The standard operational review template is included in this skill. See also `templates/report_template.md` for a ready-to-use skeleton.

## Scripts

- `scripts/gather_common_status.sh` – Collects basic system status (gateway, disk, processes) in a concise format suitable for inclusion in reports.
